definition: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that combines the strengths of retrieval-based and generation-based approaches in natural language processing, also known as Retrieval-Augmented Language Models. It leverages a vector database to store and retrieve relevant context, which is then used to augment the generation capabilities of a language model.
simple_explanation: Imagine you're trying to write a story, but instead of starting from scratch, you have a magical library that can fetch relevant books or articles related to your topic, and then you use those as inspiration to write your story. Retrieval Augmented Generation works in a similar way, where it uses a database to fetch relevant context, and then uses that context to generate text. This approach helps to improve the accuracy, coherence, and diversity of the generated text.
technical_explanation: Under the hood, RAG works by first encoding the input prompt into a dense vector using an embedding model, and then using this vector to query a vector database to retrieve relevant context, such as text chunks or documents. The retrieved context is then injected into a language model, which generates text based on the input prompt and the retrieved context, using techniques such as chunking and similarity search to ensure coherence and relevance. The engineering trade-offs in RAG involve balancing the size and complexity of the vector database, the quality of the embedding model, and the computational resources required for retrieval and generation.
investor_explanation: Robust configuration of Retrieval Augmented Generation directly affects system efficiency and business-critical accuracy metrics, driving cost savings and higher end-user retention.
